In this review, we focus on the recent processes in CAR-T cells, especially interdisciplinary strategies to solve the dilemma of current CAR-T-cell therapy, including lethal cytotoxicity, restricted trafficking, limited tumor infiltration, immunosuppressive microenvironment and limited persistence.

Lethal cytotoxicity
-------------------

Lethal cytotoxicity is the primary concern limiting the widespread clinical use of CAR-T-cell therapy. Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) is a severe supraphysiological inflammatory response triggered by proinflammatory cytokines [[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR10 "Khadka RH, Sakemura R, Kenderian SS, Johnson AJ. Management of cytokine release syndrome: an update on emerging antigen-specific T cell engaging immunotherapies. Immunotherapy. 2019;11:851–7.")]. Currently, several therapies have been developed to avoid the lethal cytotoxicity, mainly divided into “self-control” and “active-control” therapies. Furthermore, “switch on” and “switch off” CARs have been constructed through a combination of “active-control” and other controllable tools, including suicide genes and sensors of light, sound or oxygen.

### Self-control

“Self-control” reduces lethal cytotoxicity by limiting the expression or antigen affinity of CARs. One strategy to limit CAR expression is transient transfection of T cells with CAR-encoding messenger RNA (mRNA). Since CAR mRNA cannot be integrated into the genome, the number of CAR proteins expressed on the T-cell surface gradually decreases with the proliferation of T cells [[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR11 "Beatty GL, O’Hara MH, Lacey SF, Torigian DA, Nazimuddin F, Chen F, et al. Activity of mesothelin-specific chimeric antigen receptor T cells against pancreatic carcinoma metastases in a phase 1 trial. Gastroenterology. 2018;155:29–32."), [12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR12 "Foster JB, Choudhari N, Perazzelli J, Storm J, Hofmann TJ, Jain P, et al. Purification of mRNA encoding chimeric antigen receptor is critical for generation of a robust T-cell response. Hum Gene Ther. 2019;30:168–78.")]. CD5/LNP-FAP (FAP, a marker of activated fibroblasts)-CAR-T cell therapy was developed recently, in which modified FAPCAR mRNAs were packaged in CD5-targeting LNPs for transient FAPCAR expression (Fig.[2A](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). LNPs target and reprogram CD5 T cells in vivo to kill fibroblast cells for fibrosis reduction and successfully recover heart injury with limited toxicities because of the transient expression of FAPCAR, providing the possibility of editing T cells in vivo to generate tumor-targeting cells [[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR13 "Rurik JG, Tombacz I, Yadegari A, Mendez Fernandez PO, Shewale SV, Li L, et al. CAR T cells produced in vivo to treat cardiac injury. Science. 2022;375:91–6.")].

**Fig. 2: Molecular switches to control CAR-T cells.**

[![Image 7: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41419-025-07454-x/MediaObjects/41419_2025_7454_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x/figures/2)

“Self-control” CAR-T design strategy, including CAR “expression control” such as CD5/LNP-FAP CAR-T cells (**A**) and CAR “affinity control” such as EGFRCAR-T cells (**B**). “Switch off active-control” CAR-T design strategy, including TK-ganciclovir suicide system such as B7H3-sr39tk CAR-T cells (**C**), iCasp9 suicide system such as iRC9 CAR-T cells (**D**), and RTX-CD20 suicide system such as CubiCAR-T-cell therapy (**E**). “Switch-on active-control” CAR-T design strategy, including light-induced activation such as LiCAR-T cells (**F**) and TamPA-Cre system (**G**), FUS-induced activation such as FUS-CAR-T cells (**H**), and hypoxia-induced activation such as HypoxiCAR-T cells (**I**).

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x/figures/2)

As the critical factor, the affinity of CAR is another layer for “self-control”. CAR-T cells with exceptionally high affinity can cause nonspecific targeting and serious cytotoxicity [[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR14 "Vander Mause ER, Atanackovic D, Lim CS, Luetkens T. Roadmap to affinity-tuned antibodies for enhanced chimeric antigen receptor T cell function and selectivity. Trends Biotechnol. 2022;40:875–890.")]. CAT CAR (a novel CD19 CAR) shows at least 40-fold lower affinity for CD19 than FMC63 CAR (a high-affinity CAR widely used in clinical research) but exhibits stronger antitumor activity and milder neurological side effects [[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR15 "Ghorashian S, Kramer AM, Onuoha S, Wright G, Bartram J, Richardson R, et al. Enhanced CAR T cell expansion and prolonged persistence in pediatric patients with ALL treated with a low-affinity CD19 CAR. Nat Med. 2019;25:1408–14.")]. Similarly, tuning the affinity of CAR to EGFR (a TAA robustly expressed on multiple human cancers [[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR16 "Morgan RA, Johnson LA, Davis JL, Zheng Z, Woolard KD, Reap EA, et al. Recognition of glioma stem cells by genetically modified T cells targeting EGFRvIII and development of adoptive cell therapy for glioma. Hum Gene Ther. 2012;23:1043–53.")]) increases the ability of CAR-T cells to kill tumor cells rather than normal tissues with lower EGFR expression [[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR17 "Liu X, Jiang S, Fang C, Yang S, Olalere D, Pequignot EC, et al. Affinity-tuned ErbB2 or EGFR chimeric antigen receptor T cells exhibit an increased therapeutic index against tumors in mice. Cancer Res. 2015;75:3596–607.")] (Fig.[2B](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). The strategy of reducing the binding affinity between CAR and its target antigen preserves CAR-T cells’ ability to kill high antigen-expressing cancer cells rather than healthy tissues with low antigen expression.

### Active-control—“switch off”

Rapid and flexible CAR-T cell elimination is an ideal strategy to protect patients from lethal cytotoxicity. Some suicide genes, such as herpes simplex virus-thymidine kinase (HSV-TK), inducible Caspase 9 (iCasp9) and CD20, have been utilized as safety switches to conditionally ablate CAR-T cells.

For osteosarcoma targeting, the HSV-TK gene was coexpressed with B7H3 (a TAA of osteosarcoma) to engineer B7H3-sr39tk CAR T cells [[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR18 "Murty S, Labanieh L, Murty T, Gowrishankar G, Haywood T, Alam IS, et al. PET reporter gene imaging and ganciclovir-mediated ablation of chimeric antigen receptor T cells in solid tumors. Cancer Res. 2020;80:4731–40.")]. B7H3-sr39tk CAR T cells showed powerful antitumor activity in vivo. The antiviral drug ganciclovir would bind to HSV-TK and inhibit CAR-T cell DNA replication. Then, a number of activated CAR T cells declined rapidly 2 days after ganciclovir administration, without damage to normal tissues (Fig.[2C](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). Rapamycin-induced/caspase-9-based (iRC9)-CD19 and MyD88 and CD40 (iMC)-CD123 are cotransfected to generate dual-switch (DS)-CAR-T cells, providing more choices to flexibly control CAR-T-cell activity (Fig.[2D](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)) [[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR19 "Foster AE, Mahendravada A, Shinners NP, Chang WC, Crisostomo J, Lu A, et al. Regulated expansion and survival of chimeric antigen receptor-modified T cells using small molecule-dependent inducible MyD88/CD40. Mol Ther. 2017;25:2176–88.")]. Rimiducid administration activated MyD88 and CD40 signaling and led a robust ligand-dependent induction of nuclear factor κB. DS-CAR-T cells were activated and expansion in a dose-dependent manner. As a typical iCasp9 cascade, iRC9 contains three elements: FKBP12, FRB and caspase-9 [[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR20 "Duong MT, Collinson-Pautz MR, Morschl E, Lu A, Szymanski SP, Zhang M, et al. Two-dimensional regulation of CAR-T cell therapy with orthogonal switches. Mol Ther Oncolytics. 2019;12:124–37.")]. When the drug rapamycin is applied, the FKBP12-RAP-FRB ternary complex is formed, followed by dimerization and activation of caspase-9 to rapidly induce apoptosis of DS-CAR-T cells. Antibody-induced cell death is also used for CAR-T-cell elimination. CubiCAR is a trifunctional construct containing a human CD34 epitope (for cell enrichment), three CD20 mimotopes and CAR that targets BCMA+ tumor [[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR21 "Valton J, Guyot V, Boldajipour B, Sommer C, Pertel T, Juillerat A, et al. A versatile safeguard for chimeric antigen receptor T-cell immunotherapies. Sci Rep. 2018;8:8972.")] (Fig.[2E](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). FDA-approved antibody rituximab (RTX) is a chimeric, monoclonal anti-CD20 antibody and CD20-expressing cells can be efficiently eliminated by RTX. CubiCAR specifically killed the BCMA+ human tumor cell line MM.1S and then could be efficiently depleted by RTX to minimize potential lethal cytotoxicity [[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR22 "Philip B, Kokalaki E, Mekkaoui L, Thomas S, Straathof K, Flutter B, et al. A highly compact epitope-based marker/suicide gene for easier and safer T-cell therapy. Blood. 2014;124:1277–87.")].

### Active-control—“switch on”

Optogenetics is a powerful tool for controlling the cytotoxicity of CAR-T cells. Light-switchable CAR T cells (LiCARs) “sleep” in darkness and do not function unless excited by blue light [[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR23 "Nguyen NT, Huang K, Zeng H, Jing J, Wang R, Fang S, et al. Nano-optogenetic engineering of CAR T cells for precision immunotherapy with enhanced safety. Nat Nanotechnol. 2021;16:1424–34.")]. In this system, the functional domains of LiCARs are split into two halves. One domain employs bacterial peptide SsrA that is fused to the C-terminus of avena light-oxygen-voltage domain 2 (LOV2) and the other domain is conjected with binding partner SspB. The binding domain of SsrA is blocked by LOV2, which prevents it from binding to SspB. Therefore, anti-tumor therapeutic activity of LiCARs is blocked in the dark. When activated with blue light, the steric occlusion caused by LOV2 is unlocked, allowing the LOV2-SsrA and SspB to form optical dimerizer and activate LiCARs (Fig.[2F](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). Similarly, in a drug-photoactivatable “AND-gated” TamPA-Cre system, Cre recombinase is divided into two parts, CreN (2–59 aa) and CreC (60–343 aa, containing the nuclear location sequence), and then used for the construction of ERT2-CreN-nMag and NLS-pMag-CreC. CreERT2 serves as an additional security lock in addition to blue-light-inducible magnet protein (nMag, pMag) [[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR24 "Allen ME, Zhou W, Thangaraj J, Kyriakakis P, Wu Y, Huang Z, et al. An AND-gated drug and photoactivatable Cre-loxP system for spatiotemporal control in cell-based therapeutics. ACS Synth Biol. 2019;8:2359–71.")] (Fig.[2G](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). Collectively, CAR-T-cell activity is limited to the tumor site under the cooperation of tamoxifen and blue light, thus largely diminishing lethal cytotoxicity.

Focused ultrasound (FUS) can safely deliver mechanical energy into deep tissues for local heat generation, and has been widely used for adjuvant antitumor therapy [[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR25 "MacDonell J, Patel N, Rubino S, Ghoshal G, Fischer G, Burdette EC, et al. Magnetic resonance-guided interstitial high-intensity focused ultrasound for brain tumor ablation. Neurosurg Focus. 2018;44:E11.")]. FUS-CAR-T cells can be directly controlled with short-pulsed FUS stimulation [[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR26 "Wu Y, Liu Y, Huang Z, Wang X, Jin Z, Li J, et al. Control of the activity of CAR-T cells within tumours via focused ultrasound. Nat Biomed Eng. 2021;5:1336–47.")]. CD19 CAR expression is blocked by the loxP-STOP-loxP element and is triggered by heat-shock-protein promoter-mediated Cre expression, which senses the heat generated by FUS (Fig.[2H](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). A total of 29% of FUS-CAR-T cells expressed CARs after a short FUS stimulation time and 82.9% of target tumor cells were eliminated by FUS-CAR-T cells.

Hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs) respond to the low oxygen environment and result in tumor progression [[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR27 "Li T, Mao C, Wang X, Shi Y, Tao Y. Epigenetic crosstalk between hypoxia and tumor driven by HIF regulation. J Exp Clin Cancer Res. 2020;39:224.")]. HypoxiCAR-T cells are designed based on the response of hypoxia-responsive elements (HREs) to HIF1α [[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR28 "Kosti P, Opzoomer JW, Larios-Martinez KI, Henley-Smith R, Scudamore CL, Okesola M, et al. Hypoxia-sensing CAR T cells provide safety and efficacy in treating solid tumors. Cell Rep Med. 2021;2:100227.")] (Fig.[2I](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Fig2)). A cassette of 9 HRE repeats served as the CAR promoter; thus, many HypoxiCAR-expressing T cells were detected in the TME rather than normoxic tissues. To further limit off-tumor CAR T-cell activation, a 203-amino-acid oxygen-dependent degradation domain (ODD) is appended onto the C-terminus of CAR, leading to ubiquitination-dependent CAR protein degradation in normoxic tissues. This dual hypoxia-sensing system endows HypoxiCAR-T cells with features of hypoxia-restricted expression and activation of CAR in the TME and thus largely limits lethal cytotoxicity.

Restricted trafficking and limited tumor infiltration
-----------------------------------------------------

Trafficking and infiltration of T cells largely depend on the chemokines, whereas many solid tumors suppress the secretion of chemokines or secrete mismatched chemokines [[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR29 "van der Woude LL, Gorris MAJ, Halilovic A, Figdor CG, de Vries IJM. Migrating into the tumor: a roadmap for T cells. Trends Cancer. 2017;3:797–808.")]. Moreover, solid tumors are substantially different from hematological tumors, and there is a more complex structure constituting a physical barrier to T-cell infiltration, containing abundant tumor-associated fibroblasts and blood vessels [[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR30 "Junttila MR, de Sauvage FJ. Influence of tumour micro-environment heterogeneity on therapeutic response. Nature. 2013;501:346–54.")]. Here, we mainly review recent efforts to manipulate chemokines and overcome physical barriers to CAR-T infiltration.

### Chemokine-based CAR-T-cell enrichment

The activity of chemokine axes that are related to the CD8+ T-cell antitumor response generally determines the migration and infiltration capabilities of CAR-T cells. However, the activity of these chemokine axes is generally blocked in the TME, which typically prohibits the trafficking and infiltration [[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#ref-CR31 "Kohli K, Pillarisetty VG, Kim TS. Key chemokines direct migration of immune cells in solid tumors. Cancer Gene Ther. 2022;29:10–21.")]. Therefore, increasing the activity of antitumor chemokine axes serve as a promising strategy to enhance the antitumor ability of CAR-T cells (Table[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41419-025-07454-x#Tab1)).

Abstract
--------

Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell therapy has transformed cancer treatment and the field of immunotherapy. Although CAR T cell therapy has demonstrated considerable clinical success for the treatment of B cell malignancies, expanding its therapeutic efficacy and accessibility for other hematological malignancies and solid tumors remains a challenge. Key limitations include manufacturing constraints and therapeutic hurdles, such as CAR T cell persistence, proliferation, tumor trafficking and treatment-related toxicities. To overcome the unique challenges associated with CAR T cell therapy, novel technological advancements in CAR design, delivery, and T cell functionality can be leveraged. This review will explore three innovative approaches: gene editing and silencing, armoring strategies and _in vivo_ CAR gene delivery. These approaches are all aimed at enhancing the accessibility and therapeutic efficacy of CAR T cell therapy in hematological malignancies.

Introduction
------------

Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell therapy has transformed the field of immunotherapy and emerged as a groundbreaking treatment for hematological malignancies. CARs are synthetic receptors that recognize and target specific tumor antigens by delivering activation signals to T cells for effector functions. Autologous CAR T cell therapy is a personalized immunotherapy strategy that targets malignant cancer cells with specificity, contrasting the limitations of traditional anticancer therapies such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy [[1](https://www.isct-cytotherapy.org/article/S1465-3249(25)00720-0/fulltext#)

CAR T cell therapy utilizes peripheral blood mononuclear cells from a patient’s leukapheresis, and is manufactured by isolating and genetically reprogramming primary T cells _ex vivo_ with a CAR transgene, either through viral transduction or through a non-viral delivery method, such as a transposon system [[2](https://www.isct-cytotherapy.org/article/S1465-3249(25)00720-0/fulltext#)

]. The final CAR cellular product is commonly administered systemically by intravenous infusion, and in some solid tumor applications, injected locally or intratumorally. This review will explore several recent technological developments in cellular therapies, covering genetic editing, “armored” CARs, and _in vivo_ CAR T cell therapy. These technical advances aim to improve various aspects of the cellular engineering and manufacturing process; with the goal of expanding the clinical application and efficacy of CAR T cell therapies in a wider range of diseases, notably for the treatment of solid tumor cancers and autoimmune diseases.

CAR architecture is most commonly designed to have an antigen recognition domain, a hinge region, a transmembrane domain and an intracellular signaling domain. The development of CAR T cells began with the derivation of the so-called first-generation CAR, which integrates a single-chain variable fragment (scFv) from the antigen-binding domain of an antibody with an intracellular CD3ζ T cell receptor (TCR) signaling domain [[3](https://www.isct-cytotherapy.org/article/S1465-3249(25)00720-0/fulltext#)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent innovations focus on refining CAR structures to enhance binding affinity, increase specificity, and improve T cell activation, thereby boosting overall anti-tumor efficacy.
Genetic enhancements, such as CRISPR/Cas9 editing, are employed to augment CAR T cell functionality, increase persistence, and improve the ability to overcome tumor resistance.

Strategies aim to improve CAR T cell survival and infiltration in solid tumors by modifying metabolic pathways and utilizing immune checkpoint inhibitors to counteract the suppressive tumor microenvironment.
